In this chapter, we will try and understand what implication it had for the lives of students. For, the British in... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 66 OUR PASTS – III, CIVILISING THE “NATIVE”, EDUCATING THE NATION 67, 68 OUR PASTS – III, CIVILISING THE “NATIVE”, EDUCATING THE NATION 69. full_notes:
Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that civilising the “Native”, Educating the Nation 6 In the earlier chapters, you have seen how British rule affected rajas and nawabs, peasants and tribals.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will try and understand what implication it had for the lives of students.

The text explains that for, the British in India wanted not only territorial conquest and control over revenues.

The text explains that they also felt that they had a cultural mission: they had to “civilise the natives”, change their customs and values.

The text explains that how were Indians to be educated, “civilised”, and made into what the British believed were “good subjects”?

The text explains that the British could find no simple answers to these questions.

The text explains that they continued to be debated for many decades.

The text explains that how the British saw Education Let us look at what the British thought and did, and how some of the ideas of education that we now take for granted evolved in the last two hundred years.

The text explains that in the process of this enquiry, we will also see how Indians reacted to British ideas, and how they developed their own views about how Indians were to be educated.

The text explains that the tradition of Orientalism In 1783, a person named William Jones arrived in Calcutta.

The text explains that he had an appointment as a junior judge at the Supreme Court that the Company had set up.

The text explains that in addition to being an expert in law, Jones was a linguist.

The text explains that he had studied Greek and Latin at Oxford, knew French and English, had picked up Arabic from a friend, and had also learnt Persian.

The text explains that at Calcutta, he began spending many hours a day with pandits who taught him the subtleties of Sanskrit language, grammar and Fig.

The text explains that 1 – William Jones learning Persian Linguist – Someone who knows and studies several languages

The text explains that 66 OUR PASTS – III Madrasa – An Arabic word for a place of learning; any type of school or college poetry.

The text explains that together with them, Jones set up the Asiatic Society of Bengal, and started a journal called Asiatick Researches.

The text explains that in this process, the British would become the guardians of Indian culture as well as its masters.

The text explains that he urged that the British government in India stop wasting public money in promoting Oriental learning, for it was of no practical use.

The text explains that education for commerce In 1854, the Court of Directors of the East India Company in London sent an educational despatch to the Governor-General in India.

The text explains that outlining the educational policy that was to be followed in India, it emphasised once again the practical benefits of a system of European learning, as opposed to Oriental knowledge.

The text explains that european learning, it said, would enable Indians to recognise the advantages that flow from the expansion of trade and commerce, and make them see the importance of developing the resources of the country.

The text explains that from Thomas Babington Macaulay, Minute of 2 February 1835 on Indian Education Source 1

The text explains that education departments of the government were set up to extend control over all matters regarding education.

The text explains that steps were taken to establish a system of university education.

The text explains that attempts were also made to bring about changes within the system of school education.

The text explains that after 1857, however, the British government in India was reluctant to directly support missionary education.

The text explains that there were no fixed fee, no printed books, no separate school building, no benches or chairs, no blackboards, no system of separate classes, no roll-call registers, no annual examinations, and no regular time-table.

The text explains that adam discovered that this flexible system was suited to local needs.

The text explains that after 1854, the Company decided to improve the system of vernacular education.

The text explains that it felt that this could be done by introducing order within the system, imposing routines, establishing rules, ensuring regular inspections.

The text explains that it appointed a number of government pandits, each in charge of looking after four to five schools.

The text explains that teaching was now to be based on textbooks and learning was to be tested through a system of annual examination.

The text explains that pathshalas which accepted the new rules were supported through government grants.

The text explains that those who were unwilling to work within the new system received no government support.

The text explains that over time, gurus who wanted to retain their independence found it difficult to compete with the government aided and regulated pathshalas.

The text explains that in the earlier system, children from poor peasant families had been able to go to pathshalas, since the timetable was flexible.

The text explains that the discipline of the new system demanded regular attendance, even during harvest time when children of poor families had to work in the fields.

The text explains that you will read about some of these efforts in Chapter 8.

The text explains that how would you have responded to the coming of the new system of government- regulated pathshalas ?

The text explains that can you think of the various possible reasons for this fact?  In a speech delivered on January 15, 1908 in Bombay, Aurobindo Ghose stated that the goal of national education was to awaken the spirit of nationality among the students.

The text explains that aurobindo Ghose emphasised that although the students should remain connected to their own roots, they should also take the fullest advantage of modern scientific discoveries and Western experiments in popular governments.

The text explains that i would therefore begin the child’s education by teaching it a useful handicraft and enabling it to produce from the moment it begins its training … I hold that the highest development of the mind and the soul is possible under such a system of education.

The text explains that only every handicraft has to be taught not merely mechanically as is done today but scientifically, i.e. the child should know the why and the wherefore of every process.

The text explains that 79 As nationalist sentiments spread, other thinkers also began thinking of a system of national education which would be radically different from that set up by the British.

The text explains that cIVILISING THE “NATIVE”, EDUCATING THE NATION 75 that childhood ought to be a time of self-learning, outside the rigid and restricting discipline of the schooling system set up by the British.

The text explains that many individuals and thinkers were thus thinking about the way a national educational system could be fashioned.

The text explains that some wanted changes within the system set up by the British, and felt that the system could be extended so as to include wider sections of people.

The text explains that (c) Mahatma Gandhi thought that promotion of literacy was the most important aim of education.

The text explains that (d) Rabindranath Tagore felt that children ought to be subjected to strict discipline.

The text explains that why did William Jones feel the need to study Indian history, philosophy and law?

The text explains that why did James Mill and Thomas Macaulay think that European education was essential in India?

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 6: Weavers Iron Smelters and Factory Owners
